one_line_takeaway: LLM vulnerability-detection ability was systematically underestimated because benchmarks stripped away code context; when callee/data-flow context and rationale-checking are added (CORRECT), SOTA models hit 67% accuracy and ~0.8 precision -- though recall (~0.5) and rare-CWE generalization remain the real bottlenecks.
key_finding: With sufficient context, the three consensuses are overturned. (Finding #1) SOTA DeepSeek-R1 reaches 67% accuracy and F1 ~0.6 in Strict Mode with a (1,0) pairwise proportion of 37%, vs the 25% random baseline -- versus F1 0.5-0.6 / accuracy 0.5-0.55 in the context-free 'w/o context, w/o revision' setting. (Finding #3) 671B models (ds-r1, ds-v3) reach precision near 0.8 with a (1,1) false-alarm-on-patch proportion of only ~10%, refuting the 'insensitive to patches' claim. (Finding #4) Most false positives are NOT genuine inability to distinguish patched code: 'Patch Ignored' is rare (reasoning model 9.5% vs non-reasoning 37.5%); most FPs come from the model seeing the patch but wrongly deeming it insufficient. (Finding #5) A clear model-size scaling law appears once context is added, hidden before. (Findings #6-7) Test-time scaling exists but is uneconomical -- o3-mini-high uses ~5x the thinking tokens of o3-mini-medium for <0.05 accuracy gain (power law); sequential scaling ('overthinking') erodes recall while parallel scaling (self-consistency) is more stable.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CORRECT (Context-Rich Reasoning Evaluation of Code with Trust), an evaluation framework (explicitly NOT a detection tool) for LLM-based software vulnerability detection. Its thesis is that three widely held community 'consensus' beliefs -- that LLMs are (1) unreliable at vuln detection, (2) insensitive to patches, and (3) performance-plateaued across model scales -- are artifacts of 'context-deprived' evaluations that judge LLMs on isolated functions/files. CORRECT supplies rich execution/data-flow context (callee functions, macros, type declarations, global variables, slicing paths/parameters) around each target function, elicits both a binary HAS_VUL/NO_VUL verdict and a natural-language rationale via zero-shot CoT, then uses an LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o) to check whether the rationale actually identifies the ground-truth root cause (not just the right label). The authors' own contribution is (a) the framework/dataset and (b) the empirical re-evaluation overturning the three consensuses; the three consensuses themselves and their supporting numbers (e.g. GPT-4 (1,0)=12.94% vs 22.7% random from Ding et al.) are prior/cited work being critiqued, not this paper's results.
threat_model: This is an evaluation-methodology and benchmarking paper, not an attack or defense paper, so there is no adversary in the traditional sense. The security context is detecting real-world software vulnerabilities (CVEs/CWEs) in C/C++ code before they are exploited; the 'problem' the paper targets is measurement validity -- prior benchmarks may mis-measure LLM vuln-detection ability because they omit the cross-function/cross-file context that governs whether code is actually vulnerable. The two failure modes it formalizes are UO(I) Incorrect Conclusion (right rationale, wrong label due to missing callee context, e.g. CVE-2017-7875 where erealloc actually aborts on failure) and UO(II) Incorrect Rationale (right label, wrong reason -- flagging a non-ground-truth code region). No proposed defense against a live attacker; no adversarial-input threat model is claimed or invented.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) the LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o) is not infallible -- validated at 92% (91/99) accuracy on a 50-case manual sample over ds-v3/ds-r1; (2) complete context is impossible to gather for all real-world programs, so some vulnerabilities remain under-contextualized. Reviewer-noted caveats: scope is C/C++ function-level CVE pairs only; the headline results (RQ1-RQ2) run on a 400-pair CWE-1000 subset rather than the full 2,000-pair dataset; three 'Rare' CWE buckets are tiny (CWE-435=10, CWE-697=10, CWE-693=30 pairs), so per-CWE F1 for those (e.g. CWE-697=0.400) rests on few samples; ground-truth patch labels are assumed correct (98% audited on 50 pairs); r1-qn-7b was dropped for anomalous outputs; and recall stays low (~0.5) even for SOTA, so the framework reframes rather than solves the detection problem. This is a conference-formatted preprint (placeholder 'Conference'17' venue).

datasets_used: CORRECT dataset: 2,000 vulnerable-patched program pairs from 364 real-world projects spanning 99 CWEs; avg shared-context length 13,249 tokens, avg function-level code 1,574 tokens; 98% label accuracy (manual audit of 50 pairs)
CWE-1000 evaluation subset: 400 pairs across 10 top-level CWEs (50 pairs each, except CWE-693=30, CWE-697=10, CWE-435=10)
Source datasets the CORRECT set is built on: MoreFixes, PrimeVul, ReposVul
Tools: cflow (call graphs), Joern (Code Property Graphs)
quantitative_results: DeepSeek-R1: 67% accuracy, F1 ~0.6, (1,0) pairwise proportion 37% (Strict Mode) vs 25% random baseline
Context-free (w/o context, w/o revision) baseline: F1 0.5-0.6, accuracy 0.5-0.55, precision 0.5-0.55 (near random)
671B models (ds-r1, ds-v3): precision near 0.8; (1,1) false-alarm-on-patch proportion ~10%
False-positive attribution: 'Patch Ignored' 9.5% for reasoning model (ds-r1) vs 37.5% for non-reasoning (ds-v3); majority of FPs are 'Patch Deemed Insufficient'
Test-time scaling: o3-mini-high uses ~5x thinking tokens of o3-mini-medium for <0.05 accuracy gain (power-law); avg thinking tokens o3-mini-low/medium/high ~500+/1,000+/5,000+
Sequential scaling reduces recall ~10% (overthinking/conservatism); parallel scaling (USC over 3/5/8 rationales) more stable
SOTA recall ~0.5 vs precision ~0.8 (recall is the stated bottleneck)
13 LLMs, 4 families (Qwen, Llama, DeepSeek, OpenAI), 7B-671B; r1-qn-7b excluded for anomalous outputs; Strict Mode max_feedback_rounds=4; temperature=0
baselines_compared: Within-paper ablation baselines: 'w/o context, w/o revision' (function-level, prior-work-equivalent) vs 'w/ context, w/o revision' vs Lenient Mode vs Strict Mode

13 evaluated LLMs compared against each other: Qwen2.5-7B/14B/32B-Inst, Llama-3.1-8B/3.3-70B-Inst, DeepSeek-V3/R1 (671B), DeepSeek-R1-Distill (Qwen-7B/14B/32B, Llama-8B/70B), o3-mini (low/medium/high)
Random-guessing reference baselines: 25% for (1,0) proportion, 0.5 precision

Cited (NOT this paper's own) prior consensus results used as contrast: Ding et al./PrimeVul GPT-4 (1,0)=12.94% and (1,1)=54.26%, GPT-3.5 (1,0)=6.21% vs 22.7% random; Steenhoek et al. balanced accuracy 0.50-0.55; Yin et al. F1 0.084-0.128; Khare et al. F1 ~0.6; VulnLLMEval precision 39-57%
